author: @stevecummins324
text:
Great comparison. 

Does the ink shear thicken? (many inks do) 
If so there are potential uses for it in  speed limiters in say gravity powered generators, clockwork, or for clutches that automatically engage above certain rpms. 
Spin a disk or drum in shear thickening liquid... And as speed increases there's a point where viscosity starts to increase rapidly, and such locks disk to container until speed drops again.

author: @spex357
text: It might need some added gunk to slow its evaporation.

author: @richardteychenne3950
text: Spot on test Luke. If failures or drops in performance occur later it will probably due to the ink drying and cracking/flaking due to the hot cold cycling environment. So a question, have you done any experiments on painting ink on flexible surfaces letting it dry then flexing to check on adherence and possibility of cracking or flaking?

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: The ink adheres really well, I have tried a PET sheet about 1mm thick, once it's on there it can be very flexible. Cheers mate! :)

author: @carlswanson8041
text: Another thing you might want to test is long term running at the various benchmarks (I.E. say six to eight hours of steady operation with temperature comparisons etc to see if the variables are still significant either for good or ill.

replies.text: Awesome suggestion!  I have been running it every day doing light tasks as well as doing my edits. I should record the temps during this. Cheers mate! :)

author: @AdrianP247
text:
Hi Luke,
Slightly different subject...
Could you use your conductive ink to prime an object with non-conductive surface (like stone or plastic) so you could electro-plate to object?
Would it work? 
Would the ink wash off in the plating "bath" process?
Would the copper/nickel/"whatever metal" bond with the ink during electro-plating.
Keep up the good work, and good content.
thanks.

they've been there, done that, it works.

replies.text:
Luke hasn't directly lasered kapton tape to make carbon tracks in the standard intercalated fingered capacitor style yet.. 
then gone one better than anyone else by actually dipping it in the ink to massively improve it's performance as it bonds to the less conductive carbon tracks and not the kapton, wipe it with sparing wd before lasering

author: @charlesdickens6706
text: ....I get the feeling it's perhaps cold in your region of the planet.

author: @charlesdickens6706
text: .....once it was beryllium oxide that was all the rage, it was suddenly withdrawn from sale  . Hardly anyone had known how poisonous it was . It was acclaimed as the best heatsink compound . Silver powder paste then came in for a period . Silver was too peculiar in that it would form growth fingers under electrical fields that would reach into the inside of semiconductors and all components altering or destroying them . In the thermionic period which was a very high voltage period the silver mica capacitors would too often become diodes . The silver coating of valve sockets and pins had to be discontinued for aviation standards ....it remains to be seen if graphene won't misbehave .

author: @stevetobias4890
text: Interesting comparison. The ink will be a bitch to remove once it's been in there awhile won't it. It will be dry and I'm not sure if isopropyl alcohol will remove it, but I don't know the contents apart from graphite powder.

replies.author: @paulmaydaynight9925
replies.text:
it will be grabby (that's good, use a Stanley blade to break the seal after a drop of whatever) just like the so called brand name primarily oil based formulations after they dry out, for those just a drop of 3in1 or wd40.

 this ink was the usual water base i assume, and wasn't burnished on first, so a drop of soaked water and elbow grease if your into changing the stuff every year, most just set and forget

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: @paulmaydaynight9925 the new ink is water resistant which means it will need the WD40 or similar.

author: @NorthernKitty
text: Impressive!!  I wasn't aware of the thermal benefits of that ink.  Is there a substance you could mix it with to turn it into more of a paste without diminishing the properties?  So that you could apply it without worrying about it going places you don't want it to go?

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: I was thinking this at the beginning, and things like glycerol came to my mind. I just decided to try it with just the ink. it spreads really nicely you just gotta watch the amount, don't want to put too much on. I think once it's on there it's on there, I put maybe 1-2ml worth, and it's still performing really well. Thank you! :)

author: @ryanjamesloyd6733
text: I might try doping the usual stuff with the ink, to ensure continued good contact and keep it from drying out.

author: @htmagic
text: Luke, really love that steampunk computer in the rear! One thing to worry about the conductive ink is that if it migrates off the top of the chip and drip onto the board, it may short out some traces. Is the thermal paste thicker and less likely to run or migrate, especially under warm conditions? If not, you may have a new market for your conductive ink! Cheers!

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: These were my thoughts as well,  I thought I would try just the ink first, that way if something like that did happen I could use something to thicken it up. It is doing super well at the moment though.  Cheers mate! :)

replies.author: @htmagic
replies.text: @MyNameIsLukey Cool! you may have a winner on your hands! If it does work out, contact Linus TechTips, another YouTuber and let him do a review on it. You may not be able to keep up with the demand! ;-)

author: @kreynolds1123
text: nice! I've used dirt cheap automotive lithium grease for decades.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Awesome! That is amazing, I wonder what else you could use. I know if I ever run out of thermal paste I will be using lithium grease or conductive ink depending on how this experiment goes lol. Cheers mate! :)

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: @MyNameIsLukey i had forgotten to mention I used automotive lithium grease.  but to your question what else can be used? In short, you want it to squese all the air out, and not to change size through drying out, nor melt or be too runny. You want as little as possible getting the two metal surfaces close as possible.

author: @roberthopgood1894
text:
When you do cooling tests
Also include the ambient room temperature to determine the delta temps
sometimes the ambient room temp will be different so that a higher room temp will give a higher cpu temp

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Thank you for the suggestion mate, I will make sure to include in the update video. Cheers! :)

author: @hootsmin
text: In order to load the CPU well you should use a Prime95 torture test or something similar, 3dmark isn't that great at loading the CPU.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Prime95? I never heard of that. I'm assuming it's a proper CPU stress test. I will look it up 100% because I'm not interested in the graphical capabilities at the moment just the CPU stuff. Thank you for the suggestion mate!

replies.author: @hootsmin
replies.text: @MyNameIsLukey I work in IT, forever we have used Prime95 to stress CPU's and to determine if the machine is stable after overclocking.  I don't overclock anymore, rather I prefer to undervolt, but stress testing a machine for stability is still mandatory after building, if it can Prime for 24 hours error free it is pretty much solid as a rock.  For GPU's I would typically use Furrmark for getting up to temperature and stress testing, 3dmark or similar is useful also though.  You want the task manager open when your doing the tests so you can see the CPU is loaded (all-cores pegged at 100%).

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: @hootsmin Thank you mate! I knew there were programs out there, but these seem really good to go with. I will definitely take a look.  Cheers!

author: @totherarf
text:
Just a minor point ..... are both the ink and the paste Electrically conductive?
If so you are squashing a conductive gell across an electrical device ..... Not that there could be any ramifications from that ;0)

Also do you have a die to apply the two substances under test? If not how can you get the comparison between them? 
Obviously the time factor will be valid ... but the test as a whole will only be subjective. 
A further thought occurs ..... does your computer throttle the speed due to heat? ... if it does it may be worth checking as when throttled (if it is) it will be generating less heat thus skewing the result.

Of course none of these may actually be relevant here ;0)
Nice video Luke.

author: @dudebroham4083
text: I know this is off topic but I had a question or suggestion for Mister Murray. I was wondering if anyone had tried to add carbide to a hydrogen generator to make a mixture of hydrogen and acetylene. I just searched for the subject here on YouTube and I couldn't find anything about it. Thanks for all of your content. You guys rock!

replies.author: @paulmaydaynight9925
replies.text: see 'Carbide_lamp' if you want to give it a go, basically you are making an on demand selective derivative of so called brown's gas and producing a lot of heat you could also make use of and a derivative wet paste of slaked lime as the by-products , be careful

replies.author: @dudebroham4083
replies.text: @paulmaydaynight9925 . Yeah I have been reading up on carbide and its reaction with water. I was just wondering if anybody had tried to mix acetylene with hydrogen. I have seen several videos of people mixing propane and even Coleman fuel or naphtha with hydrogen and they had some pretty good results.
